proposal.reasoning: This is the initial exploration phase with no prior data, requiring broad sampling across parameter space to identify sensitive directions. I propose 8 diverse parameter sets: (1) Baseline center values, (2) High cohesion (high ε, small rmin) to maximize density/Hvap, (3) Low cohesion (low ε, large rmin) to test lower bounds, (4) High dipole (long dummy bonds, high charges, linear geometry), (5) Low dipole (short bonds, low charges, bent geometry), (6) Flexible (low force constants), (7) Rigid (high force constants), and (8) Balanced compromise. This orthogonal sampling strategy maximizes information gain about how cohesive parameters (ε, rmin), electrostatic parameters (charges, dummy bond lengths), and structural rigidity affect the target properties (density, Hvap, surface tension, dipole moment).

hypothesis.scientific_rationale: Changing epsilon (ε) and rmin controls cohesive energy and packing efficiency: increasing ε strengthens London dispersion forces enhancing density and heat of vaporization, while decreasing rmin allows tighter packing. The dipole moment is governed by the separation between DUP and DUN dummy beads (controlled by bond lengths ACY_DUP_bl and DIM_DUN_bl) and charge magnitude (DUP_charge/DUN_charge). Linear geometries (θ=180°) maximize separation while bent geometries (θ=90°) minimize it. By systematically varying these across 8 forks, we can map the parameter landscape and identify which combinations produce the target liquid properties (ρ≈0.94 g/cm³, ΔHvap≈11 kcal/mol, γ≈32 dyn/cm, μ≈3.7 D).
hypothesis.expected_benefit: Forks 2-3 will reveal the sensitivity of density and surface tension to cohesive parameters. Forks 4-5 will calibrate the dipole moment response to charge and geometry changes. Forks 6-7 will assess how flexibility affects dynamics and thermodynamics. This will identify promising parameter regions for refinement in subsequent iterations.
hypothesis.test_method: Evaluate all 8 parameter sets in parallel MD simulations, measuring density, heat of vaporization, surface tension, and dipole moment at 298K and 313K to validate the hypothesis and identify the optimal parameter region.

This flexibility allows surface molecules to adopt orientations that reduce surface tension and restore proper temperature dependence (decreasing with increasing T), counteracting the surface-tension-increasing effect of higher epsilon values. Additionally, slight reductions in partial charges combined with increased dummy bond lengths maintain the target dipole moment while reducing excessive interfacial polarity contributions.
